coredata.dc:title: Full-color-emission carbon quantum dots by controlling surface states in a system of solvent
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yaoyao
Peng, Kangliang
Deng, Yakun
Zhao, Youjun
Ai, Jinshui
Min, Xiao
Hu, Mengzhu
Huang, Shuai
Yu, Lixin
coredata.dc:description: carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely studied due to their outstanding merits. In this article, it is important and interesting that the full-color-emission (blue, green and red light) CQDs are successfully obtained by a simple one step method. They are synthesized from two common precursors, m-phenylenediamines and phosphorus acid, using a one pot solvent engineering strategy. These CQDs can emit bright and stable light under a single-wavelength UV light. Moreover, the alterations of photoluminescence (PL) emission of the blue, green, red are ascribed to the surface states but not the particle size. The redshifted emission of the three CQDs samples is tentatively proposed to result from the contents of sp2-hybridized domains, surface CO and the pyridinic N.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Full-color-emission
Surface states

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation for the full-color fluorescent CQDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully thank the financial supports of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51762033 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic diagram of full-color fluorescent CQDs. (A) A solvent engineering strategy for the preparation of full-color fluorescent CQDs from mPD and H3PO4. (B) the fluorescent photographs under 365 nm UV light.

(A) Corresponding normalized PL emission spectra of the five samples; PL emission spectra of (B) B-CQDs, (C) G-CQDs, (D) R-CQDs excited at different wavelengths. (E) CIE color coordinates; (F) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the three types of CQDs.
Time-resolved PL decay curves of the three types of CQDs.
TEM images (left) of (a) B-CQDs, (b) G-CQDs, (c) R-CQDs and their corresponding particle size distributions (right).
(A) XRD patterns, (B) FTIR spectra of the three CQDs.
High-resolution C1s, N1s, and O1s XPS spectra of the three CQDs samples: (A) B-CQDs, (B) G-CQDs, (C) R-CQDs. Each band is deconvoluted following the literature.
Dependence of the HOMO and LUMO energy levels of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, R-CQDs.
The calculated values of radiative and non-radiative lifetime for the three CQDs.

head.ce:title: Full-color-emission carbon quantum dots by controlling surface states in a system of solvent

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: : College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, Nanchang, 330031, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of materials Science and Engineering, Nanchang University, 999 Xuefu Road, Nanchang 330031, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of materials Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been widely studied due to their outstanding merits. In this article, it is important and interesting that the full-color-emission (blue, green and red light) CQDs are successfully obtained by a simple one step method. They are synthesized from two common precursors, m-phenylenediamines and phosphorus acid, using a one pot solvent engineering strategy. These CQDs can emit bright and stable light under a single-wavelength UV light. Moreover, the alterations of photoluminescence (PL) emission of the blue, green, red are ascribed to the surface states but not the particle size. The redshifted emission of the three CQDs samples is tentatively proposed to result from the contents of sp2-hybridized domains, surface CO and the pyridinic N.

ce:section.ce:para: CQDs are considered as zero-dimensional materials composed of ultrasmall carbon core and oxygen functional groups on the surface with particle size less than 10 nm [1,2]. They have been attracted increasing research interest since being discovered by accident during separation of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [3,4], due to the unique optical, chemical, and physical properties such as high stability, biocompatibility [5], low cost, toxicity [6], easy functionalization and so on. Consequently, CQDs have been applied in different fields including data security [7,8], fluorescent sensors [9,10], light-emitting diodes [11,12], solar photovoltaics [13,14], photocatalysis [15,16], etc, which indicate that they might replace the traditional semiconductor quantum dots in the future. In addition, as a newly developing nanomaterial, diverse synthetic methods have been identified and solvothermal techniques represent the simple and efficient strategy for the preparation of CQDs [17,18].

To our best knowledge, the PL emissive mechanisms are still controversial because the obtained CQDs products have complex composition. Moreover, the multicolor fluorescence emission of CQDs is a vital requirement to explore the PL mechanisms. To data, several studies have been done on the fabrication of multicolor CQDs. Ding et al. synthesized CQDs with a tunable PL from blue to red and suggested by the surface oxidation [19]. Wang et al. reported a facile method for the preparation of the full-spectra emission CQDs, which has been attributed to the combined effects of the particle size and the sp2-hybridized carbon [20]. Ding et al. produced a solvothermal method towards CQDs with a tunable PL ranging from 443 (blue) to 745 (red) nm, ascribed to the synergistic effect among the p-electron system, graphitic N, and surface states [21]. These studies offer valuable insight into the PL mechanisms and highlight the need for further research.

Herein, we report a simple and cheap one-step solvothermal engineering strategy to modulate fluorescence color of CQDs, having remarkably optical properties from blue to red by using m-phenylenediamine and phosphorus acid as starting materials, Moreover, the further detailed studies clarify the luminescence mechanisms to explain the full color emission. The PL emission of redshift is mainly determined by the synergistic effect with an increase of the number of conjugated sp2-domains, CO groups and a decrease of pyridinic N.

The m-phenylenediamine (mPD, 99%) is purchased from Aladdin reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Formamide (FOR, AR) is provided by Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. Phosphorus acid (H3PO4, 85 wt %) is obtained from XiLong Scientific Co., Ltd., Guangdong, China. Ethyl acetate (EA, AR), ethanol (EtOH, AR) are purchased from DaMao Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin, China. Acetone (DMK, >99.5%) is purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China. All chemicals reagents are used without further purification. Deionized water is used in the whole experimental procedures.

The preparation of the full-color fluorescent CQDs can be demonstrated a one-step solvent engineering method. Firstly, mixture aqueous solutions of mPD, H3PO4 and different solvent are sealed in a Teﬂon-lined autoclave, heated up to a certain reaction temperature oven keeping for a certain reaction time, and then cooled to room temperature naturally. Next, the obtained suspensions are centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 10 min to remove precipitation (Washing three times) and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. At last, the solutions can be further purified via dialysis, freeze drying to remove solvent and dispersed in water for further characterization.

The microstructure and morphologies of the as-synthesized CQDs are characterized by Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL). The PL excitation and emission spectra (recorded by Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer) and the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra (obtained by using HITACHI UH4150 spectrophotometer) are used to analyze the optical performance. The absolute PL quantum yield (PLQY) and the Time-resolved PL decay curves are performed using a full functional steady-state and the FS5 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd., UK). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are carried out by a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffraction machine using Cu Kα (1.54 Å) radiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. The structure and composition are investigated by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Spectrum 10.0) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250X) spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation for the full-color fluorescent CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: In previous reports, solvents can affect the extents of dehydration and carbonization processes between reactants [22]. Based on this observation, multi-color-emissive CQDs can be synthesized by choose different solvents. But many prepared solutions emit blue and dim green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV light in strong acid (Fig S1). Consequently, as show in Fig. 1 , it can be seen that the schematic diagram. The full-color fluorescent CQDs are synthesized by using mPD and H3PO4 as the precursor and modifier at different solvents (FOR, EtOH, DMK, EA, H2O) (Fig. 1A). The obtained CQDs (under 365 nm UV light) with blue, green, yellowish-green, yellow, red fluorescence from left to right (Fig. 1B) are marked as B-CQDs, G-CQDs, YG-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs, respectively. For B-CQDs, G-CQDs, YG-CQDs and Y-CQDs, mPD (0.1 g), H3PO4 (1 mL) and 40 ml solvents are transferred into a Teﬂon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 24 h. Notably, the R-CQDs are prepared using a procedure similar to the describe above for the other CQDs. But the reaction temperature and time are 200 °C and 2 h, respectively.

The optical properties of the CQDs samples aqueous solution are analyzed by the PL spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. First, the normalized PL emission spectra of B-CQDs, G-CQDs, YG-CQDs, Y-CQDs and R-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2 A. The five CQDs samples display entirely different emission behavior and the strongest blue, green, yellowish green, yellow and red emission peaks at about 455, 513, 545, 566, 608 nm, respectively. The results imply that the full color emission (blue, green and red) is successfully obtained. In following discussions, the B-CQDs, G-CQDs and R-CQDs samples are detailed studied for convenience. Synchronously, for the convenience of practical analysis, the three typical CQDs samples (B-CQDs, G-CQDs, R-CQDs) PL emission spectra excited at different wavelengths as shown in Fig. 2B–D. As the excitation wavelength is increased, the PL emission peaks of B-CQDs (Fig. 2B) show an obvious red shift. According to Fig. 2C and D, for G-CQDs and R-CQDs, the emission bands centers are observed and their position hardly changes under different excitation wavelengths. It should be noted that the rule of the dependence of excitation wavelength is different with the B-CQDs, which can be attributed to the changes of band gap due to the variety of the size. As known, when the size distribution of CQDs is relatively inhomogenous, the energy gaps become wide, indicating that the transition mode is more [23]. Moreover, the Commission Internationale de L'Eclairage (CIE) color coordinates of the three samples are acquired from the PL emission spectra as present in Fig. 2D, showing the CIE coordinates are (0.154,0.158) for B-CQDs, (0.262,0.608) for G-CQDs, and (0.621,0.379) for R-CQDs.

Meanwhile, the UV–Vis spectra are employed to investigate the energy absorption characteristic of CQDs as shown in Fig. 2F the UV–Vis spectra of B-CQDs (blue line) present only one band-edge absorption at about 400 nm. Notably, For G-CQDs (green line) and R-CQDs (red line), display different absorption both UV region (200–350 nm) and the lower energy region. In the UV region, a single peak is observed at 257 nm for G-CQDs and 276 nm for R-CQDs, which are corresponding to aromatic π−π* transition of CC bond. At longer wavelengths, the spectra exhibit a strong excitonic absorption bands at 438 (G-CQDs) and 534 (R-CQDs) nm assigned to n−π* transition related to the CO, CN bonds [19,20].

Moreover, The absolute PLQY are estimated to be 9.86% (B-CQDs), 21.66% (G-CQDs), 2.89% (R-CQDs) by using integrating sphere (Please see Table S1 in Supporting Information). Generally, the key to the high PLQY is the reduction of non-radiation trap centers. Synchronously, to further understand the excited-state radiative relaxation dynamics of the three types of CQDs. The time-resolved PL decay curves and the corresponding lifetimes are shown in Fig. 3 and Table S2, respectively. The three CQDs samples are excited at 370 nm, and the emission is monitored at 455 nm, 513 nm, 608 nm for B-CQDs, G-CQDs and R-CQDs, respectively. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves are fitted with a double exponential function, with the average decay lifetimes (τavg) are 5.9 ns for B-CQDs, 7.7 ns for G-CQDs and 2.5 ns for R-CQDs. According to the excited state lifetime theory, the fluorescence lifetimes are mainly determined by the radiative transition (τr) and non-radiative transition lifetimes (τnr). The values of them are calculated by using the PLQY and the τavg, shown in Table 1 . The firstly increase and then decrease trend of τnr of 6.55 ns for B-CQDs, 9.83 ns for G-CQDs, and 2.57 ns for R-CQDs is similar to the trend of PLQY and the τavg. Similar to those in previous reports, indicating that the obtained CQDs possess excellent optical properties and unique tunable fluorescence emission [24,25].

To understand the origin of the tunable optical properties, the morphologies of the B-CQDs, G-CQDs and R-CQDs are observed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 4 , the images reveal that the three CQDs samples are uniform and monodispersed nanoparticles (shown on the corresponding size histograms according to the statistically analyzed results from 30 nanoparticles on the right of Fig. 4), with average sizes of around 2.59 nm (B-CQDs), 2.72 nm (G-CQDs), and 2.92 nm (R-CQDs), respectively. The results demonstrate that the size of the three CQDs is nearly same, imply that the quantum confinement effect and size effect are not the major factor in color variation of CQDs.

The XPS findings are used to further clarify the surface compositions of the three CQDs. From the full survey XPS spectra (Fig. S2 in Supporting information), the three typical peaks at 285 eV, 400 eV and 532 eV are observed and attributed to C1s, N1s, O1s, respectively [19]. The results illuminate that the samples are composed of the same elements, which is also proved by FTIR results (In Fig. 5B). Fig. 6 displays the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O 1s spectra of the three CQDs. All of the C 1s spectra can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.1 eV and 288.5 eV, which are corresponding to sp2 C (C–C/CC), sp3 C (C–O/C–N) and the carbonyl carbons (CO) characteristic. Moreover, the whole N 1s band display three kinds of species, relevant to pyridinic N (398.4 eV), amino N (399.4 eV), and pyrrolic N (400.3 eV), respectively. Conspicuously, the relative content of pyridinic N is decreased gradually from B-CQDs to R-CQDs, which can be consistent with the FTIR results. In the O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra, the O 1s band is divided into two peaks at 530.9 and 532.3 eV for CO and C–O, respectively [22]. As listed in Table S3 in the Supporting information, the increases of the atomic ratio between oxygen and carbon from 10.4% to 33.7% as the C content is decreased from 85.19% to 71.66% for B-CQDs, G-CQDs, R-CQDs suggest a major increasing degree of oxidation in the surface. From Table S4, it can be seen that the contents of the C species are evaluated. Simultaneously, the percentage of sp3 C (C–O/C–N) depresses from 19.6 to 11.4 as the sp2 C (C–C/CC) content is increased from 78.3% to 84.8% from B-CQDs to G-CQDs, which implies a increasing degree of graphitization. For R-CQDs, the content of the sp2 C (77.9%) is the smallest and the sp3 C (16.9%) is between those of the B-CQDs and G-CQDs. And more notably, the amount of CO groups becomes increased from 2.1 to 5.2% for B-CQDs to R-CQDs, reflecting that the red emission is attributed to the increased of CO groups on the surface of CQDs.

A comparison of the structural characterization results of the three CQDs samples clearly demonstrated two important observations. One is the redshifted emission is attributed to the increased sp2-hybridized domains. The carbonization degree is increased when the sp3-hybridized carbon in the precursors partially is transformed into sp2-hybridized domains in B-CQDs and G-CQDs. Furthermore, the surface emission centers are resulted from the increased surface CO groups for B-CQDs to R-CQDs, leading to the redshift of the emission. The other is that the blueshift of emission mainly attributed to the pyridinic N, which probably creates capture of excitons and lead to changes in the surface states [36].

Although the mechanisms of the PL emissions of CQDs are not fully clear, their amazing PL is a powerful motivation for continuing explore. In previous report, the quantum size effects and surface states have been regarded as the key factors for the optical property regulation. For CQDs, a larger size reduces the bandgap with a corresponding increase in PL emission wavelength. The increase in the graphene domains within CQDs leads to greater delocalization and more uniform distribution of energy, reducing the energy gap between the highest unoccupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). In addition, as these surface moieties (coordination bonds on the carbon backbone and the adjacent chemical groups) possess different energy levels, some researchers agree that the fluorescence emission can be determined by the surface states of CQDs. In this case, however, the three CQDs samples exhibit nearly identical in size, demonstrating that the quantum size effects are not considered the dominant factors for the redshift of the CQDs PL. Meanwhile, the PL redshifts are mainly attribute to the surface states induced by conjugated sp2 domains (including the surface groups containing oxygen and nitrogen) creating new energy levels between the HOMO and LUMO. The energy gap of the three CQDs samples are calculated utilizing the equation Egopt = 1240/λ edge, where λedge is the wavelength of the maximum absorption edge. As show in Fig. 7 , the calculated values are decreased gradually from 2.97 to 2.06 eV from B-CQDs to R-CQDs. Thereby tuning the emission wavelength effectively.

In summary, a facile approach of a one-step solvothermal route is used to prepare the full-color emission CQDs. The resulting CQDs possessed bright and stable luminescence in solvents. The PLQYs of the different CQDs with blue, green and red emission reached 9.86%, 21.66% and 2.89%, respectively. Furthermore, the FTIR, XPS measurements demonstrate that the different surface states of the three CQDs samples are resulted from different sp2-conjugated domains, CO groups and pyridinic N contents, ultimately determining the different bandgap and colors of emission. The simple analysis further confirms the major optical properties of the CQDs and represents the importance of the full-color emission CQDs, which imply a wide application in optics, electronics, and biotechnology fields in the future.

Yaoyao Yang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft preparation. Kangliang Peng: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Yakun Deng: Resources, Software. Youjun Zhao: Visualization. Jinshui Ai: Investigation. Xiao Min: Methodology. Mengzhu Hu: Formal analysis. Shuai Huang: Project administration. Lixin Yu: Supervision, Validation. We confirm that the manuscript has been read and approved by all named authors and that the order of authors listed in the manuscript has been endorsed by all of us. We understand that the Corresponding Author is the sole contact for the Editorial process. He is responsible for communicating with the other authors about progress, submissions of revisions and final approval of proofs. Signed by all authors as follows: Yaoyao Yang, Kangliang Peng, Yakun Deng, Youjun Zhao, Jinshui Ai, Xiao Min, Mengzhu Hu, Shuai Huang, Lixin Yu*.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully thank the financial supports of National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51762033).
